questions.question:
In the figure shown above, each inside square is formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of the next larger square. The area of the smallest square (shaded) as shown, in cm^2, is:

A. 12.50
B. 6.25
C. 3.125
D. 1.5625
questions.to_find: Area of the smallest (innermost shaded) square

understand.plain: A large square has side 5 cm (area 25 cm^2). Each successive inner square is formed by joining the midpoints of the outer square's sides. Each time you do this, the new square's area is exactly half the previous square's area. Four nested squares are shown; the smallest (third inner square, fourth overall) is shaded.

keywords.explain: A square formed by connecting the midpoints of the four sides of an existing square; its diagonal equals the side of the outer square, so its area is half.
keywords.example: A 5x5 square (area 25) produces a midpoint square of area 12.5.

keywords.explain: Each successive midpoint square has area exactly half the previous one, because the new side = old side / sqrt(2), so new area = old area / 2.
The sequence of areas forms a geometric sequence with common ratio 1/2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 260' width='260' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='220' height='220' class='bg'/><rect x='75' y='20' width='110' height='110' fill='none' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5' transform='rotate(45,130,130)'/><polygon points='130,42.5 207.5,130 130,207.5 52.5,130' fill='none' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='130,91.25 168.75,130 130,168.75 91.25,130' fill='none' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='130,110.625 149.375,130 130,149.375 110.625,130' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Side of outer square = 5 cm</text><text x='130' y='15' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Nested midpoint squares</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four nested squares, each formed by joining midpoints of the previous, with the innermost shaded.

given.aim: Find the area of the smallest (innermost) shaded square

terms.meaning: The side length of the largest square, which is 5 cm
terms.connects: Starting value for the halving sequence

terms.meaning: Each inner square is formed by joining the four midpoints of the outer square's sides
terms.example: Midpoints of a 5x5 square are at distance 5/sqrt(2) apart

terms.meaning: There are 4 squares total (outermost + 3 inner); the innermost (4th) is shaded
terms.connects: Determines how many times to halve the area

given.plan: Start with the outermost square of side 5 cm (area 25). Each midpoint square has half the area. Apply the halving rule three times to reach the innermost square.

steps.title: Area of outermost square
steps.note: The largest square has side 5 cm as given in the figure.

steps.title: Apply the midpoint-halving rule repeatedly
steps.note: Each successive midpoint square has area = half of the previous. The innermost is the 4th square, so divide by 2^3 = 8.

solution.result: Area of the smallest shaded square = 25/8 = 3.125 cm^2

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula needed; the key identity is that a midpoint square has area exactly half its parent, derived from the fact that the new side = old side / sqrt(2).

questions.question:
Equal sized circular regions are shaded in a square sheet of paper of 1 cm side length. Two cases, case M and case N, are considered as shown in the figures below. In the case M, four circles are shaded in the square sheet and in the case N, nine circles are shaded in the square sheet as shown. What is the ratio of the areas of the unshaded regions of case M to that of case N?

A. 2 : 3
B. 1 : 1
C. 3 : 2
D. 2 : 1
questions.to_find: Ratio of unshaded area in case M to unshaded area in case N

understand.plain: A unit square (1 cm x 1 cm) contains circles that fit perfectly inside it arranged in a grid. In case M, a 2x2 grid of equal circles is packed in the square; in case N, a 3x3 grid of equal (but smaller) circles. The key insight is that in both cases the total shaded (circular) area is the same fraction of the square, so the unshaded areas are equal.

keywords.explain: The fraction of the square's area covered by circles. For an n x n grid of circles filling a 1x1 square, the radius of each circle is 1/(2n), and the total area of n^2 circles is n^2 * pi * (1/(2n))^2 = pi/4, independent of n.
keywords.example: For n=2: 4 circles each of radius 1/4, total area = 4 * pi/16 = pi/4. For n=3: 9 circles each of radius 1/6, total area = 9 * pi/36 = pi/4.

keywords.explain: Area of the square minus total area of all circles: 1 - pi/4, the same for both cases.
keywords.example: Case M unshaded = 1 - pi/4; Case N unshaded = 1 - pi/4.

keywords.explain: Circles are equal-sized within each case (all 4 circles in M are the same; all 9 in N are the same), but circles in M are larger than those in N.
keywords.example: Radius in M = 1/4 cm; radius in N = 1/6 cm.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 180' width='360' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='150' height='150' class='bg'/><circle cx='47.5' cy='57.5' r='37.5' class='hi'/><circle cx='122.5' cy='57.5' r='37.5' class='hi'/><circle cx='47.5' cy='132.5' r='37.5' class='hi'/><circle cx='122.5' cy='132.5' r='37.5' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Case M (4 circles)</text><rect x='200' y='20' width='150' height='150' class='bg'/><circle cx='225' cy='45' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='275' cy='45' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='325' cy='45' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='225' cy='95' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='275' cy='95' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='325' cy='95' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='225' cy='145' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='275' cy='145' r='25' class='hi'/><circle cx='325' cy='145' r='25' class='hi'/><text x='275' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Case N (9 circles)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left: a square with a 2x2 grid of large circles. Right: a square with a 3x3 grid of smaller circles. Unshaded corners in both are equal.

given.aim: Find ratio of unshaded area in M to unshaded area in N

terms.meaning: Both cases use a square of side 1 cm, so total area = 1 cm^2
terms.connects: The base area from which circle area is subtracted

terms.meaning: Four circles fit in a 2x2 grid in a 1x1 square, so each circle has radius = 1/(2*2) = 1/4 cm
terms.example: Total area of 4 circles = 4 * pi * (1/4)^2 = pi/4 cm^2
terms.connects: Gives shaded area for case M

terms.meaning: Nine circles fit in a 3x3 grid in a 1x1 square, so each circle has radius = 1/(2*3) = 1/6 cm
terms.example: Total area of 9 circles = 9 * pi * (1/6)^2 = 9 * pi/36 = pi/4 cm^2
terms.connects: Gives shaded area for case N, identical to case M

given.plan: Compute total circle area for each case. The unshaded area = square area - circle area. Compare the two.

steps.title: Shaded area in case M (4 circles in 2x2 grid)
steps.formula_raw: Total circle area = n^2 * pi * r^2, where r = 1/(2n)
steps.note: Each of the 4 equal circles has diameter = half the square side, so radius = 1/4.

steps.title: Shaded area in case N (9 circles in 3x3 grid)
steps.formula_raw: Total circle area = n^2 * pi * r^2, where r = 1/(2n)
steps.note: Each of the 9 equal circles has diameter = one third the square side, so radius = 1/6.

steps.title: Unshaded areas and their ratio
steps.note: Both cases have identical shaded fractions (pi/4), so their unshaded areas are equal.

solution.result: Ratio of unshaded area (M) to unshaded area (N) = 1 : 1

questions.formula_note: The core insight is that for an n x n arrangement of equal circles in a unit square, total circle area = pi/4 regardless of n, making the unshaded regions equal in both cases.

questions.question:
A square has side 5 cm smaller than the sides of a second square. The area of the larger square is four times the area of the smaller square. The side of the larger square is _______ cm.

A. 18.50
B. 15.10
C. 10.00
D. 8.50
questions.to_find: Side length of the larger square

understand.plain: Let the side of the smaller square be x and the side of the larger square be x + 5. The area of the larger is 4 times the area of the smaller. Setting up the equation (x+5)^2 = 4x^2 leads to a quadratic. Solving gives x = 5, so the larger square has side 10 cm.

keywords.explain: The ratio of areas of two squares equals the square of the ratio of their sides.
keywords.example: If one side is twice the other, the area is four times as large.

keywords.explain: An equation of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0, solved by factoring or the quadratic formula.
The larger square's side exceeds the smaller square's side by 5 cm.

understand.visual_alt: Two squares: the smaller with side x and the larger with side x+5, where the larger has 4 times the area.

given.aim: Find the side of the larger square

terms.meaning: Let x = side of the smaller square (in cm)
terms.connects: Used to set up the relationship between the two squares

terms.meaning: Side of the larger square = x + 5 (5 cm more than the smaller)
terms.example: If x = 5, larger side = 10, area = 100 cm^2
terms.connects: The quantity we want to find

terms.meaning: Area of larger square = 4 * area of smaller square
terms.connects: The constraint that lets us solve for x

given.plan: Set up the equation (x+5)^2 = 4x^2. Expand, rearrange into a standard quadratic, and solve for x. Take the positive root, then add 5 to get the larger square's side.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.note: Larger square side = x + 5; area of larger = 4 times area of smaller.

steps.title: Rearrange into standard quadratic form
steps.note: Move all terms to one side.

steps.title: Solve the quadratic
steps.apply: a=3, b=-10, c=-25 | discriminant = 100 + 300 = 400 | x = (10 +/- 20)/6 | x = 30/6 = 5 (positive root) or x = -10/6 (rejected)
steps.note: Only the positive root x = 5 is physically meaningful (side length must be positive).

steps.title: Find the larger square's side
steps.note: Verify: area of larger = 100, area of smaller = 25, ratio = 4. Correct.

solution.result: Side of the larger square = 10.00 cm

questions.formula_note: The quadratic-formula registry entry is used to solve 3x^2 - 10x - 25 = 0, yielding the positive root x = 5 and therefore a larger square side of 10 cm.

questions.question:
Which of the following assertions are CORRECT?

P: Adding 7 to each entry in a list adds 7 to the mean of the list
Q: Adding 7 to each entry in a list adds 7 to the standard deviation of the list
R: Doubling each entry in a list doubles the mean of the list
S: Doubling each entry in a list leaves the standard deviation of the list unchanged

A. P, Q
B. Q, R
C. P, R
D. Q, S
questions.to_find: Which assertions about the effect of list transformations on mean and standard deviation are correct

understand.plain: Mean is a linear average, so adding a constant shifts it by that constant, and multiplying all values by a constant scales the mean by the same factor. Standard deviation measures spread (distances from the mean). Adding a constant to all values shifts the entire dataset uniformly, so deviations from the mean do not change, leaving SD unchanged. Doubling all values doubles the deviations, so SD is also doubled. Hence P and R are correct; Q and S are both wrong.

keywords.explain: Sum of all entries divided by the count. Adding a constant c to every entry shifts the sum by n*c, so the mean shifts by c. Multiplying every entry by k scales the sum by k, so the mean scales by k.
keywords.example: List {1,2,3}: mean = 2. After adding 7: {8,9,10}, mean = 9 = 2 + 7. After doubling: {2,4,6}, mean = 4 = 2 * 2.

keywords.explain: Measures spread: sqrt of average squared deviation from the mean. Adding a constant shifts mean and all values equally, so deviations are unchanged and SD stays the same. Multiplying by k scales all deviations by k, so SD also scales by k.
keywords.example: List {1,2,3}: SD = sqrt(2/3). After adding 7: SD unchanged. After doubling: {2,4,6}, SD = 2 * sqrt(2/3).

keywords.explain: For transformation x' = a*x + b: new mean = a*(old mean) + b; new SD = |a| * old SD (SD is not affected by the additive shift b).
keywords.example: Doubling (a=2, b=0): mean doubles, SD doubles. Shifting (a=1, b=7): mean shifts by 7, SD unchanged.

understand.visual_alt: A table showing how mean and standard deviation change under adding a constant and multiplying by a constant.

given.aim: Identify which of P, Q, R, S are true statements

terms.meaning: Adding 7 to each entry adds 7 to the mean
terms.connects: Mean is linear; shift in data shifts the mean by the same amount

terms.meaning: Adding 7 to each entry adds 7 to the standard deviation
terms.example: SD of {1,3,5} = SD of {8,10,12} = sqrt(8/3). Adding a constant changes mean but not spread. FALSE.
terms.connects: SD measures deviation from mean; a uniform shift does not change deviations

terms.meaning: Doubling each entry doubles the mean
terms.connects: Mean scales with the data values

terms.meaning: Doubling each entry leaves the standard deviation unchanged
terms.example: SD of {1,3,5} = sqrt(8/3); SD of {2,6,10} = 2*sqrt(8/3). Doubling scales SD by 2. FALSE.
terms.connects: SD scales with the magnitude of deviations, which double when values double

given.plan: Apply the rules for how mean and standard deviation transform under x -> x + c (shift) and x -> 2x (scaling). Identify which of P, Q, R, S are true.

steps.title: Effect of adding c to every entry
steps.formula_raw: new mean = old mean + c; new SD = old SD
steps.apply: Adding 7: new mean = old mean + 7 (P is TRUE). New SD = old SD, not old SD + 7 (Q is FALSE).
steps.note: A uniform shift moves all values equally, so all deviations from the new mean are identical to those from the old mean.

steps.title: Effect of multiplying every entry by k
steps.formula_raw: new mean = k * old mean; new SD = |k| * old SD
steps.apply: Doubling (k=2): new mean = 2 * old mean (R is TRUE). New SD = 2 * old SD, not unchanged (S is FALSE).
steps.note: Multiplying every entry by 2 also multiplies every deviation by 2, so SD doubles.

steps.title: Identify correct assertions
steps.apply: P is TRUE, Q is FALSE, R is TRUE, S is FALSE. Correct set = P and R.

solution.result: Assertions P and R are correct. Answer: C. P, R

questions.formula_note: No special registry formula needed; the standard properties of mean (linear) and standard deviation (invariant under shift, scales under multiplication) are applied directly.
